mnemonics.title: SWAK for Weak Entities
mnemonics.content: S-W-A-K: Select Weak entity, add owner entity's Primary Key as Foreign Key, Attributes become columns, Key is the partial key combined with FK

mnemonics.title: 1-1-1 Rule for Binary 1:1
mnemonics.content: Choose ONE side, add the OTHER side's Primary Key as Foreign Key (the one with total participation gets chosen)

mnemonics.title: Many Side Takes PK for 1:N
mnemonics.content: For 1:N relationships, the 'Many' side gets the Primary Key of the 'One' side as Foreign Key

mnemonics.title: New Table for M:N
mnemonics.content: Many-to-Many relationships get their OWN table with both entities' Primary Keys forming a composite Primary Key

mnemonics.title: DERIVED: Never Store, Always Compute
mnemonics.content: D-E-R-I-V-E-D: Don't Ever Represent In Real World - store Virtual Derived Entities Temporarily - Derived attributes are NOT stored in relational schema

mnemonics.title: MULTI: Separate Table for Multi-valued
mnemonics.content: M-U-L-T-I: Make Unique table, list original PK, each value gets its own row

mnemonics.title: ISA Mapping - Three Options
mnemonics.content: Option A: Separate tables for each entity (all 3 tables share PK), Option B: Only subclass tables with type attribute in superclass table, Option C: Single superclass table with type and all attributes

mnemonics.title: N-ary Relationships
mnemonics.content: N-ary relationships ALWAYS get a new table with all participating entity Primary Keys forming a composite Primary Key (unless one entity is 'owning' side in 1:N)

acronyms.usage: Unique identifier for each tuple/row in a relation, cannot be NULL for strong entities
Primary Key of another relation stored to establish relationship, enables referential integrity
The conceptual model diagram that needs to be mapped to relational schema
Represents 'is-a' relationship between entities (e.g., Employee IS-A Person), mapped using one of three strategies
Constraint ensuring FK points to existing PK, enforced through foreign key integrity rules
All minimal superkeys, one becomes PK; important when choosing which key to use as FK

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangle (entity) becoming a table (box). Each attribute becomes a column inside the box. Underline the PK column.
Think of a child holding hands with parent - the parent's identity (PK) travels with the child (weak entity) as an anchor
Imagine a bridge connecting two islands (entities). One island's passport (PK) is stamped onto the other island's visitor record (FK)
Think of one parent spreading its ID to many children - like a tree trunk (1) sending branches (N) that carry the trunk's identity
Two-way intersection creates a roundabout (new table) where both roads' identifiers are recorded at entry points
Think of expanding a single cell into a separate mini-table, like taking items out of a box and listing each on its own row
Break apart a bundle - treat the composite like a nested box, flatten it by storing each simple component as separate column

analogies.analogy: Think of an entity set as a category like 'Students' - mapping to a relation is like creating a spreadsheet where each student gets one row
Just as every person has a unique fingerprint, every tuple needs a unique PK - no two rows can have the same PK value

analogies.concept: Foreign Key as ID Card from Another Department
analogies.analogy: When an employee joins a project, they carry their employee ID (FK) into the project table - linking back to their original identity in the Employee table
If every Department MUST have at least one Employee (total participation), it's like being in a country where citizenship is mandatory - you cannot exist without belonging
A weak entity is like a child - they have their own attributes (name, age) but cannot exist independently without a parent (owner entity)

analogies.concept: Mapping Rules as Translation Between Languages
analogies.analogy: ER model is like French, Relational model is like English - mapping is the translation dictionary converting nouns (entities) and verbs (relationships) from one language to another

Students enroll in Courses - one student in many courses, one course has many students. You cannot store both in one row, so you create an Enrollment table (like a ledger) recording which student is in which course
